note: Content source of truth is the chart-chooser skill. The 6-stage filtering algorithm lives in chart-chooser.js; this file holds the questions, taxonomy, and message text.

questions.title: What does your data look like?
questions.help: Pick the row that best matches the columns you have.

options.label: One number per category
options.desc: e.g., average score for each region

options.label: Counts of one category
options.desc: e.g., how many schools fall in each region

options.label: A number over time
options.desc: e.g., score by year (one or more series)

options.label: Two numbers per record
options.desc: e.g., poverty % and score for each school

options.label: One number, many records
options.desc: e.g., the score for every school (a distribution)

options.label: A number split by two categories
options.desc: e.g., score by region AND locale

options.label: A number tied to places
options.desc: e.g., absenteeism by county, or schools on a map

options.label: Nested categories with a size
options.desc: e.g., region → district → school enrollment

options.label: Survey / rating-scale responses
options.desc: e.g., strongly agree → strongly disagree

questions.title: What should your audience take away?
questions.help: The message matters more than the data type. Pick the main point.

options.label: Compare categories
options.desc: How do these groups differ?

options.label: Show a ranking or order
options.desc: Who is top / bottom?

options.label: Show change over time
options.desc: What is the trend?

options.label: Show parts of a whole
options.desc: What share does each piece make up?

options.label: Show a distribution
options.desc: How are values spread out?

options.label: Show a relationship
options.desc: Do two things move together?

options.label: Show size or magnitude
options.desc: How big is each value?

options.label: Show difference from a baseline
options.desc: Above/below target, or the gap between two groups

options.label: Show a geographic pattern
options.desc: Where is it higher or lower?

questions.title: Who is the main audience?
questions.help: This sets how familiar the chart forms should be.

options.label: General public
options.desc: Parents, community, legislators, media — keep it familiar

options.label: School & district staff
options.desc: Principals, coaches — comfortable with a bit more

options.label: Analysts & researchers
options.desc: Fluent in charts — full toolbox available

questions.title: About how many categories or series?
questions.help: Color can distinguish about five things before it breaks down.

options.label: Just one
options.desc: A single value or series
Color-coding is fine here
Too many for separate colors

options.label: 16 or more
options.desc: Needs aggregation or small multiples

questions.title: Where will this live?
questions.help: Interactivity changes what works for many categories.

options.label: Static
options.desc: Slide, PDF, printed report — one frame tells the whole story

options.label: Dashboard
options.desc: Tableau / web — the user can hover, filter, click to highlight

messages.colorLimit: You have more than five categories. Don't give each its own color — discriminability breaks down fast past five hues. Instead: highlight one in color with the rest in grey, use small multiples (one panel each), or show a Top-N with the rest grouped as "Other."
messages.smallMultiples: With this many categories, small multiples are usually the best move — repeat the same chart once per category in a grid, each panel one color. They're under-used and they avoid color overload entirely.
messages.highlightOne: In a dashboard, plot every category in grey and let the user click or hover to highlight one — "spaghetti with a spotlight." In a static piece, pre-select the one category that carries your message.
messages.delta: Your message is about the difference itself. Plot the difference directly (a diverging bar of the gap, or a line of the gap over time) rather than two series the reader has to subtract by eye — a constant gap looks like it shrinks at higher values.
messages.survey: For rating-scale data, a diverging stacked bar (centred on neutral, disagree to the left, agree to the right) is the standard. With four or more response levels, separate bars on a common baseline read more precisely. Never use a pie for survey data.
messages.stackedSegments: Keep stacked bars to 2–3 segments. With four or more, only the bottom segment sits on a common baseline — the rest float and can't be compared precisely. Use separate bars on a common baseline (grouped bars or small multiples) instead.
messages.covidGap: If your time series spans 2019–2021, remember NC issued no School Performance Grades in 2020 and 2021. Break the line at the gap — never connect through missing years.

taxonomy.name: Vertical bar chart
taxonomy.why: Length on a common baseline is one of the most accurate things the eye can read. The default for comparing a value across a small-to-moderate number of categories.
tips: Always start the axis at zero
Sort by value unless categories have a natural order
Direct-label bars when there are 7 or fewer
One color is enough — position does the work
taxonomy.watch: More than ~15 categories crowd the labels — switch to a horizontal bar or dot plot.
taxonomy.evidence: Cleveland & McGill: position/length rank near the top of the encoding hierarchy.

taxonomy.name: Horizontal bar chart
taxonomy.why: Sorted horizontal bars show rank explicitly and give long category labels room to breathe.
tips: Start at zero
Sort descending (highest on top)
Highlight the top or bottom item with an accent color if telling a story
taxonomy.watch: If the natural order of categories matters more than rank, keep that order instead of sorting.

taxonomy.name: Dot plot
taxonomy.why: Position on a common scale is the single most accurate read. Ideal when differences are small or a zero baseline isn't meaningful (e.g., percentages in the 60–90 range).
tips: Sort by value
Horizontal layout for long labels
Light gridlines for reference
No need for a zero baseline
taxonomy.watch: When sheer magnitude is the point, bars convey "bigness" better.

taxonomy.name: Lollipop chart
taxonomy.why: A lighter bar: the stem keeps the zero baseline while the dot marks the value, reducing ink when there are many categories.
tips: Anchor stems at zero
Sort by value
Horizontal for many categories
taxonomy.watch: Less familiar to general audiences; when magnitude is the focus a plain bar is clearer.

taxonomy.name: Dumbbell chart
taxonomy.why: Two points per category joined by a line make the gap between them — before/after, or two groups — the visual focus.
tips: Horizontal layout
Color the two endpoints distinctly and label them
Sort by gap size or by one endpoint
taxonomy.watch: More than two points per category gets cluttered fast.

taxonomy.name: Line chart
taxonomy.why: The default for trends over time — the connecting line emphasises continuity and direction of change.
tips: Direct-label line ends instead of a legend
Break the line at missing years — never connect through a gap
Limit to ~5 colored series; beyond that use small multiples or highlight-one
A zero baseline isn't required, but note it if truncated
taxonomy.watch: Never use a line for non-time categorical data — lines imply continuity that isn't there.
taxonomy.evidence: Position + slope; high-accuracy reads for both level and rate of change.

taxonomy.name: Small multiples
taxonomy.why: Repeat the same chart once per category on a shared scale. The cleanest way to show many categories without color overload — and badly under-used.
tips: Keep every panel on the same scale
One color across all panels
Order panels meaningfully (by value, region, etc.)
taxonomy.watch: Panels get too small to read past ~20–30 categories.

taxonomy.name: Slope chart
taxonomy.why: Two time points joined per item — shows both the values and the direction/size of change, and highlights rank reversals.
tips: Label both endpoints directly
Highlight items that cross
Use one neutral color, or highlight a focal item against grey
taxonomy.watch: More than two time points → use a line chart. More than ~12 items → too many crossing lines.

taxonomy.name: Area chart
taxonomy.why: Emphasises cumulative volume of a trend over time; stacked areas show composition changing over time.
tips: Start at zero
Order stacked series largest on the bottom
Use transparency or stacking for multiple series
taxonomy.watch: Hard to read precise values; overlapping series obscure each other.

taxonomy.name: Sparklines
taxonomy.why: Word-sized trend lines embedded in a table — show the shape of many series at once without taking chart space.
tips: No axes or labels — just the line
Mark the start/end point
Same y-scale across the whole set
taxonomy.watch: Sacrifices precise values for density.

taxonomy.name: Pie chart
taxonomy.why: Fine for part-to-whole with five slices or fewer, and genuinely effective for "which is biggest?" Familiar to everyone.
tips: Five slices maximum — group the rest as "Other"
Order slices largest to smallest from 12 o'clock
Label slices directly with their percentage
taxonomy.watch: Never more than five slices, never for comparing similar sizes, never 3-D, never over time.

taxonomy.name: 100% stacked bar
taxonomy.why: Shows proportional composition across categories when the overall pattern — not precise segment sizes — is the message.
tips: Best at 2–3 segments
Put the most important segment on the common baseline (bottom)
Direct-label segments when space allows
taxonomy.watch: With 4+ segments the middle floats — switch to separate bars on a common baseline.

taxonomy.name: Treemap
taxonomy.why: Compact view of a large, nested set — good for spotting the dominant pieces at a glance.
tips: Label the largest rectangles
Use color for a second variable or to group
Include a size cue
taxonomy.watch: Area is imprecise; hard for similar-sized rectangles. Unfamiliar to general audiences.

taxonomy.name: Waffle chart
taxonomy.why: A 10×10 grid where each square is 1% — engaging and countable. Great for a single "X out of 100" message.
tips: 100 cells, fill from one corner
Label the headline percentage
Keep to a few parts
taxonomy.watch: Not for many categories or precise multi-way comparison.

taxonomy.name: Histogram
taxonomy.why: Shows the shape of one numeric variable — where values cluster, and whether the spread is skewed or bimodal.
tips: Start the count axis at zero
Try 10–30 bins — too few hides shape, too many adds noise
No gaps between bars (continuous data)
taxonomy.watch: For categorical counts use a bar chart, not a histogram.

taxonomy.name: Box plot
taxonomy.why: Compares several distributions at once — median, quartiles, and outliers side by side.
tips: Show outlier points
Horizontal for many groups
Add a one-line note on what the box means for tier-2 audiences
taxonomy.watch: Hides bimodal shapes; quartile conventions need explaining for general audiences.

taxonomy.name: Violin plot
taxonomy.why: Shows the full density curve per group — reveals shape (skew, bimodality) a box plot would hide.
tips: Overlay points or an inner box plot
Horizontal for many groups
taxonomy.watch: Unfamiliar below analyst audiences; unreliable for small samples.

taxonomy.name: Beeswarm / strip plot
taxonomy.why: Plots every individual point without overplotting — shows clusters, gaps, and outliers directly.
tips: Jitter or swarm to avoid overlap
Color by group
Pair with a box plot for summary stats
taxonomy.watch: Breaks down with very large samples.

taxonomy.name: Scatterplot
taxonomy.why: The default for the relationship between two numbers — shows direction, strength, and outliers via position on two scales.
tips: Add a trend line if the relationship is the message
Label outliers directly
Annotate to tell the story
taxonomy.watch: If one variable is categorical, use a dot plot or bar instead.
taxonomy.evidence: Position on two common scales — the most accurate read, in two dimensions.

taxonomy.name: Bubble chart
taxonomy.why: A scatterplot with a third measure encoded as bubble area.
tips: Scale by area, not radius
Include a size legend
Use transparency to handle overlap
taxonomy.watch: Area is imprecise; keep to ~30 bubbles or fewer.

taxonomy.name: Heatmap
taxonomy.why: Shows patterns in a matrix of values (e.g., region × year) — good for spotting clusters and trends.
tips: Use a sequential or diverging brand palette
Add cell values when the matrix is small
Mind the dark-is-more convention
taxonomy.watch: Color is the least precise encoding — not for reading exact values.

taxonomy.name: Grouped bar chart
taxonomy.why: Compares values across categories and across 2–4 groups at once, bars side by side.
tips: Zero baseline
Color groups consistently (≤5 colors)
Switch to small multiples beyond 3–4 groups
taxonomy.watch: Too many groups per cluster becomes unreadable; for proportions use a stacked bar.

taxonomy.name: Bullet chart
taxonomy.why: Actual value against a target/benchmark in a compact bar — a better dashboard gauge.
tips: Grey background bands for qualitative ranges
Dark bar for actual, line for target
Label directly
taxonomy.watch: Needs a meaningful benchmark and a familiar audience.

taxonomy.name: Pictograph (icon array)
taxonomy.why: Makes a number tangible — "73 of 100 schools." Each icon is one unit; strong for lower-numeracy audiences.
tips: Uniform icon size
Color-fill to show the proportion
Label the total and the highlighted count
taxonomy.watch: Only for small, round numbers.

taxonomy.name: Diverging bar chart
taxonomy.why: Values above and below a baseline (e.g., change from last year), with a zero line as the natural anchor. The go-to for "graph the delta."
tips: Zero baseline in the centre
Color positive vs negative (teal for good, rust for bad)
Sort by value or natural order
taxonomy.watch: If all values are positive, a plain bar is clearer.

taxonomy.name: Diverging stacked bar
taxonomy.why: The standard for rating-scale data — centred on neutral, disagree extends left and agree extends right. Best for comparing response patterns across questions.
tips: Centre on the neutral category
Use a diverging palette (rust → grey → teal)
Label segment percentages where they fit
taxonomy.watch: Middle segments lack a common baseline; the centred layout can confuse general audiences.

taxonomy.name: Choropleth map
taxonomy.why: Shades areas by value — familiar, and good for broad geographic patterns.
tips: Sequential palette with 4–6 steps
Always include a companion bar/dot chart for precise comparison
Mind that large rural areas dominate visually
taxonomy.watch: Color is imprecise; big low-population areas can mislead. Use for area data, not point data.

taxonomy.name: Dot-density map
taxonomy.why: Plots each location as a dot — shows where things are and how concentrated, without the large-area bias of a choropleth.
tips: Small uniform dots
Color by category if useful
Include geographic reference features
taxonomy.watch: Hard to read precise counts; dots overlap when very dense.
